message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 11
Recent changes: Added multi-item summation; rune building-block logic (3 alike at 60G)

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start the Refactor phase by loading architecture memory and reviewing the current state of the code.

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- No refactoring (justified): hardcoded `{ results: [{ premium: 115 }] }` is canonical "fake it" baby step; mass=2 is minimal; no duplication yet to extract; `runScenario` name matches contract.
5	
6	## After cycle 2
7	- Change: extracted module constants `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER = 1.1`, `ADMIN_FEE = 5`, and `BASE_PRICE: Record<string, number> = { sword: 100, amulet: 60 }`; replaced ternary `itemType === "amulet" ? 71 : 115` with formula `Math.round(BASE_PRICE[itemType] * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`; destructured `steps` from cast scenario.
8	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent) > APP. Opaque computed literals 71/115 hid the spec's pricing model (base × first-insurance loading + admin fee); now the domain is visible. `Math.round` neutralises IEEE-754 drift (100*1.1 = 110.000…01) for the integer-premium contract; spec uses explicit `ceil(...)` only on discounted prices.
9	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE` lookup table (extend with new item types: staff, potion, rune, moonstone). `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER` (the 1.10 loading for customers with no prior contract). `ADMIN_FEE` (the flat +5 added after all multipliers).
10	
11	## After cycle 3
12	- Change: added `staff: 80` to `BASE_PRICE`; renamed `itemType` binding to `item` (now `const item = steps[0].items[0]`, then `BASE_PRICE[item.type]`).
13	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Upcoming tests attach modifiers (`cursed`, `enchantment`) to the same item object; naming the whole item rather than just its `type` field prepares the binding to carry those properties without rework. Mass effectively unchanged (~20). Resisted extracting `priceForItem`/`quotePremium` helpers — speculative without a duplication driver (Rule 4).
14	- Naming established: `item` as the unit modifiers attach to (will gain `.cursed`, `.enchantment` shortly).
15	
16	## After cycle 4
17	- No refactoring (justified): `potion: 40` test absorbed entirely by the cycle-2 `BASE_PRICE` lookup table — zero edits to `runScenario` body. No new conditional, no duplication, no naming drift; mass unchanged (~37). Resisted extracting `priceForItem(item)` / `quotePremium(...)` again — still speculative; the cursed/enchantment/loyal-customer/multi-item tests next in line will provide the real driver for where pricing logic should live, and premature extraction would lock the shape before those forces appear.
18	
19	## After cycle 5
20	- Change: renamed local binding `cursedMult` → `cursedMultiplier`; structure of `runScenario` body unchanged (pipeline: base lookup → item-modifier multiplier → first-insurance loading + admin fee).
21	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Module constants are spelled out in full (`FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`, `CURSED_MULTIPLIER`, `ADMIN_FEE`); the local mirrored their role so it should mirror their spelling. Next cycle adds `enchantmentMultiplier` — having two siblings with consistent naming will make the "apply item modifiers" stage read uniformly and signal the extraction point cleanly when a third modifier arrives. Resisted extracting `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` now: only one modifier exists, so there is no duplication yet (Rule 3 needs a driver; Rule 4 says don't add an abstraction until forced). Mass change negligible (~34, binding name length is free).
22	- Naming established: locals that mirror module-level `*_MULTIPLIER` constants use the full word `Multiplier`, not `Mult`.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: added `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER = 1.3`; extracted helper `itemModifierMultiplier(item: Item): number` that combines `cursed` and high-enchantment factors (each `<cond> ? MULTIPLIER : 1`, multiplied together); renamed `itemBase` → `itemPrice`; `runScenario` body collapses to base lookup → item-modifier multiplier → first-insurance loading + admin fee, with the modifier stage now a single named call.
26	- Rationale: Rule 3 (No Duplication) finally has a driver — the second sibling modifier landed in cycle 6, matching the cycle-5 prediction, so the speculative extraction resisted then is now justified. Rule 2 (Reveal Intent): naming the "apply all item-attribute multipliers" stage makes the pricing pipeline scannable; `itemPrice` is more accurate than `itemBase` once modifiers have been applied. APP rose slightly (~36 → ~38) from the function-call frame, but Rule 2 > APP per project conventions. Cycle 7 (cursed AND enchantment 5) will exercise both branches together with zero edits to `runScenario` body — both factors will simply multiply inside the helper. Cycle-7's `ceil(...)` in the spec formula (220 vs would-be-rounded 220) will need attention: current `Math.round` may give same answer for that specific test by coincidence (100×1.5×1.3×1.1 = 214.5 → round→215, ceil→215; +5 = 220 either way... actually round(214.5)=214 banker's rounding in some langs, but JS Math.round = 215; ceil = 215; both yield 220), so cycle 7 may pass with no change; cycle 9 (`ceil(60 × 0.80 × 1.10) + 5 = 58`; product = 52.8, both round and ceil → 53, +5 = 58 — also coincidence-passes). The real ceil-vs-round driver only appears when loyalty discount lands on a non-`.5` fractional that diverges; defer the round→ceil switch until a test actually fails.
27	- Naming established: `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` is THE place to add new item-attribute multipliers (dragon-material reimbursement in claims will live elsewhere — claim-side, not premium-side; this helper is premium-only). `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER` (the >=5 threshold version; if a "low-enchantment" tier ever appears, rename accordingly).
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- Change: extracted `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value: number): number` that wraps `Math.ceil(Number(value.toFixed(10)))`; `runScenario` premium line collapses to `ceilInMhpcoFavor(itemPrice * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`.
31	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The spec phrase "round in MHPCO's favour" now appears in the code; the `.toFixed(10)` drift guard (which prevents IEEE-754 noise like `100 * 1.10 = 110.00000000000001` from bumping mathematically-integer prices up a euro) is no longer a magic incantation inlined mid-formula. Module mass roughly unchanged: the `runScenario` body lost ~3 (one constant + two invocations) which moved into the helper. Pre-positions for cycles 8–9 (loyalty discount: `100 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 88`, integer; `60 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 52.8`, non-integer needing real ceil) and cycle 10 (rune: `25 × 1.10 = 27.5`, ceil → 28) — every one of those is a `ceilInMhpcoFavor(...)` call now, no further inlining.
32	- Naming established: `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value)` is THE round-up gate for any euro-denominated price after multiplicative steps; use it for the final premium computation and any nested ceiling steps the spec calls out. ADMIN_FEE is added AFTER the ceiling, not inside it (spec: ceil applies to the priced value, then flat +5).
33	
34	## After cycle 8
35	- No refactoring (justified): `LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER = 0.8` follows the established `*_MULTIPLIER` module-constant convention; local `loyaltyMultiplier` mirrors it per cycle-5 naming rule; the `customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= 2 ? LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER : 1` ternary sits at `runScenario` level (NOT inside `itemModifierMultiplier`, whose scope stays item-attribute-only per cycle-6). Resisted extracting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)`: only one customer-side multiplier exists today, so Rule 3 has no driver and Rule 4 says don't add a frame without one. The real extraction driver is the upcoming "two-quote scenario … second amulet quote = 60 × 0.85 + 5" test (cycle-2 forecast), which introduces both a second customer-side modifier AND a quote-index dependency — extracting now with only `customer` as parameter would lock in the wrong signature and need rework. Cycle 9 (loyal amulet, 60 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 52.8 → ceil 53 + 5 = 58) is finally the test where `ceilInMhpcoFavor` (cycle 7) does real work — round vs ceil diverge on a non-`.5` fractional; existing helper handles it with zero edits. Mass essentially unchanged (~41).
36	
37	## After cycle 9
38	- Change: extracted module constant `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD = 2`; replaced magic `>= 2` in the loyalty ternary with `>= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD`.
39	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The `2` was the last unnamed numeric in the pricing pipeline — every other parameter (1.10, 1.50, 1.30, 0.80, 5) is a named module constant; the loyalty threshold was the odd one out. The spec's domain phrase "loyal customer ≥2 years" now reads in the code. Mass impact negligible (+1 binding, -1 constant from inline literal moving to named binding). Still resisting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)` extraction (cycle-8 reasoning unchanged — the upcoming two-quote test will force a non-customer-only signature, so extracting now locks in the wrong shape). Cycle 10 (rune 33) is a pure `BASE_PRICE` table addition — zero edits to `runScenario` predicted.
40	- Naming established: numeric thresholds that gate a multiplier follow the `*_THRESHOLD` suffix (sibling to `*_MULTIPLIER`); both stand together to read as "if customer crosses THRESHOLD, apply MULTIPLIER".
41	
42	## After cycle 10
43	- Change: reordered `BASE_PRICE` entries from insertion order (sword 100, amulet 60, staff 80, potion 40, rune 25) to descending-by-value (sword 100, staff 80, amulet 60, potion 40, rune 25). `runScenario` body untouched. Cycle-10 delta itself (`rune: 25`) was absorbed by the cycle-2 lookup table with zero edits to the pricing pipeline, as predicted in cycle 9.
44	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent), presentation tier. With five entries now, insertion order no longer carries meaning (sword and amulet were just the first two test cases); descending-by-value lets a reader scan the price ladder at a glance and immediately see which items are cheap (rune) vs premium (sword). Zero behavioural risk — `Record<string, number>` key order doesn't affect lookup semantics. Mass unchanged (~42). Resisted (again) extracting `priceForItem(item)` / `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)`: cycles 11–13 (building blocks: three alike runes pool to one amulet-priced unit) will fundamentally change how items contribute to the base price — `priceForItem` with an `Item` signature locks in the wrong shape because building blocks operate on item *groups*, not single items. Cycle 14 (two-quote scenario with post-first-contract discount 0.85) introduces a quote-index dependency that will force the customer-discount extraction with the right signature. Defer both extractions until those tests provide the driver.
45	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE` table is sorted descending by value; new entries (moonstone in cycle 13) should be inserted at the correct position to preserve the price ladder.
46

content.text:
All 11 tests pass. Now let me evaluate refactoring opportunities.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation

Current bindings in `runScenario`:
- `runeCount` — clear: count of rune-type items
- `buildingBlocks` — clear: how many complete groups of 3
- `looseRunes` — clear: remainder runes not in a block
- `itemsTotal` — clear: accumulated pre-discount price
- `60` (magic) — the building-block price (=`BASE_PRICE.amulet`)

The magic `60` in `buildingBlocks * 60` is suspicious. Spec says "three alike runes form one building block at 60G". Is `60` here referring to amulet's price coincidentally, or is it the building-block price? Per the spec, "three alike runes pool to one amulet-priced unit" — they happen to share `60`, but conceptually it's a separate concept (the building-block price tier). However, for cycle 13 (three moonstones also pool to 60), this confirms the building-block is its own constant — every group of 3 alikes = 60G.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass for `runScenario`

```
const { customer, steps } = scenario as Scenario;          // 2 bindings + 1 inv = 4
const items = steps[0].items;                              // 1 bind + 1 const + ~2 nav = 4
const runeCount = items.filter(i => i.type === "rune").length;  // ~10
const buildingBlocks = Math.floor(runeCount / 3);          // ~6
const looseRunes = runeCount % 3;                          // ~4
let itemsTotal = 0;                                        // 1 bind + 1 const = 2
for (const item of items) {                                // loop 5
  if (item.type === "rune") continue;                      // cond 4
  itemsTotal += BASE_PRICE[item.type] * itemModifierMultiplier(item);  // assign 6 + ~5
}
itemsTotal += buildingBlocks * 60 + looseRunes * BASE_PRICE["rune"];   // assign 6 + ~6
const loyaltyMultiplier = ... ? LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER : 1;    // cond 4 + ~4
const premium = ceilInMhpcoFavor(...) + ADMIN_FEE;         // ~8
return { results: [{ premium }] };                          // ~3
```
Approx total mass: ~70 in `runScenario` body.

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

### Rule 2 (Reveal Intent): The magic `60`
The `60` in `buildingBlocks * 60` is opaque. Per architecture notes (cycle 6), `BASE_PRICE` is a price-ladder lookup for items. The building-block price is conceptually distinct — it's a fixed pool price for any group of 3 alike runes (or moonstones, in cycle 13). This deserves a named constant.

### Rule 3 (No Duplication)
The mix of "filter rune count, then loop excluding rune" is two passes over items with rune-special-casing in both. There's structural duplication of "rune is the type that pools". When moonstone arrives in cycle 13, this will become real duplication. Per architecture notes (cycle 10): "cycles 11–13 (building blocks: three alike runes pool to one amulet-priced unit) will fundamentally change how items contribute to the base price." This is the moment to be careful — the upcoming cycle 13 introduces moonstone with same pooling rule. The current shape *only* handles rune-pooling; cycle 12 ("four runes") is absorbed (1 block + 1 loose); cycle 13 ("three runes + three moonstones") forces generalization.

Should I extract a "groupable types" concept now? Let me reason: cycle 12 is straight rune (no change needed). Cycle 13 needs both rune AND moonstone pooled separately. Extracting a `priceForItemGroup(type, count)` now would speculate the API; better to wait for cycle 13 to provide the actual generalization driver. The current code uses a single hardcoded type check on "rune" — that's the very thing cycle 13 will force to change. So today's refactoring scope: only the naming, not the structure.

### Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)
The two-pass structure (filter then loop) could collapse to one pass, but it's currently readable. Not the priority.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring — Extract `BUILDING_BLOCK_PRICE`

Rule 2 benefit: the formerly-magic `60` now reads as "building block price" — distinguishing it from `BASE_PRICE["amulet"]` (which also happens to be 60 but is conceptually unrelated, as the cycle-13 moonstone test will demonstrate).

## Step 8: Append to Architecture Notes

## After cycle 10
- Change: reordered `BASE_PRICE` entries from insertion order (sword 100, amulet 60, staff 80, potion 40, rune 25) to descending-by-value (sword 100, staff 80, amulet 60, potion 40, rune 25). `runScenario` body untouched. Cycle-10 delta itself (`rune: 25`) was absorbed by the cycle-2 lookup table with zero edits to the pricing pipeline, as predicted in cycle 9.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent), presentation tier. With five entries now, insertion order no longer carries meaning (sword and amulet were just the first two test cases); descending-by-value lets a reader scan the price ladder at a glance and immediately see which items are cheap (rune) vs premium (sword). Zero behavioural risk — `Record<string, number>` key order doesn't affect lookup semantics. Mass unchanged (~42). Resisted (again) extracting `priceForItem(item)` / `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)`: cycles 11–13 (building blocks: three alike runes pool to one amulet-priced unit) will fundamentally change how items contribute to the base price — `priceForItem` with an `Item` signature locks in the wrong shape because building blocks operate on item *groups*, not single items. Cycle 14 (two-quote scenario with post-first-contract discount 0.85) introduces a quote-index dependency that will force the customer-discount extraction with the right signature. input.new_string:
## After cycle 10
- Change: reordered `BASE_PRICE` entries from insertion order (sword 100, amulet 60, staff 80, potion 40, rune 25) to descending-by-value (sword 100, staff 80, amulet 60, potion 40, rune 25). `runScenario` body untouched. Cycle-10 delta itself (`rune: 25`) was absorbed by the cycle-2 lookup table with zero edits to the pricing pipeline, as predicted in cycle 9.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent), presentation tier. With five entries now, insertion order no longer carries meaning (sword and amulet were just the first two test cases); descending-by-value lets a reader scan the price ladder at a glance and immediately see which items are cheap (rune) vs premium (sword). Zero behavioural risk — `Record<string, number>` key order doesn't affect lookup semantics. Mass unchanged (~42). Resisted (again) extracting `priceForItem(item)` / `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)`: cycles 11–13 (building blocks: three alike runes pool to one amulet-priced unit) will fundamentally change how items contribute to the base price — `priceForItem` with an `Item` signature locks in the wrong shape because building blocks operate on item *groups*, not single items. Cycle 14 (two-quote scenario with post-first-contract discount 0.85) introduces a quote-index dependency that will force the customer-discount extraction with the right signature. Defer both extractions until those tests provide the driver.
- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE` table is sorted descending by value; new entries (moonstone in cycle 13) should be inserted at the correct position to preserve the price ladder.

## After cycle 11
- Change: extracted module constant `BUILDING_BLOCK_PRICE = 60` with a comment explaining that three alike "building-block" pieces (rune, moonstone, …) pool to a single unit priced at this fixed tier, independent of the per-piece `BASE_PRICE`. Replaced inline `buildingBlocks * 60` with `buildingBlocks * BUILDING_BLOCK_PRICE`. Otherwise `runScenario` body structure (rune-count → buildingBlocks/looseRunes → loop excluding rune → add pooled+loose contribution) untouched.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The bare `60` was the only remaining unnamed numeric in the pricing pipeline AND it was actively misleading — a reader could plausibly read it as `BASE_PRICE.amulet` (which is also 60), conflating two semantically distinct rules. The comment + named constant disambiguates: building-block tier ≠ amulet base. This matters because cycle 13 (three moonstones also pool to 60) will confirm the rule generalises beyond runes — moonstone base will be different, but its 3-pool price will also be 60, so the constant carries its semantics correctly across types. Mass impact ~0 (binding+invocation in vs. literal+(inline) out is a wash). Resisted (still) extracting `priceForItemGroup(type, count)` / `bundleItems(items)`: cycle 12 (four runes = 1 block + 1 loose) is absorbed by the existing block/loose split with zero changes; cycle 13 (rune AND moonstone, two pooled types) is the real driver — that's when the hardcoded `i.type === "rune"` check on lines 38 and 43 becomes a duplicated string-literal type test against a hypothetical `POOLED_TYPES` set, and the extraction signature can be designed against two concrete examples instead of one. Extracting now with only rune in mind would lock the API around a single-pooled-type assumption.
- Naming established: `BUILDING_BLOCK_PRICE` is the pool-tier price for any three-alike grouping (rune today, moonstone in cycle 13); it is intentionally separate from `BASE_PRICE` (which is per-piece) even when they share a numeric value. The "pooled types" set is currently inlined as the literal string `"rune"` in two places — cycle 13 will need to convert these to a shared `POOLED_TYPES = new Set(["rune", "moonstone"])` or equivalent.

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete.

**Naming Evaluation**: All local bindings (`runeCount`, `buildingBlocks`, `looseRunes`, `itemsTotal`, `loyaltyMultiplier`, `premium`) already reveal intent. `runScenario` matches contract. The only naming defect was the bare literal `60`.

**Refactoring Applied**: Extracted module constant `BUILDING_BLOCK_PRICE = 60` (with explanatory comment) and replaced the inline `60` in `buildingBlocks * 60`. The bare numeric was misleading — it sat next to `BASE_PRICE["rune"]` and could plausibly be misread as `BASE_PRICE.amulet` (also 60), conflating two semantically distinct pricing rules. Cycle 13 (moonstone pooling) will confirm the rule is type-agnostic.

**Mass Change**: ~70 → ~70 (literal `60` replaced by binding reference; net ~0).

**Rule Applied**: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent) > Rule 4 (Fewest Elements). Added one binding to disambiguate domain meaning.

**Resisted**: Extracting `priceForItemGroup(type, count)` or generalising the rune-specific check to a `POOLED_TYPES` set — cycle 13 is the proper driver (two pooled types will provide the actual extraction signature; doing it now with only rune locks in the wrong shape).

**Tests**: All 11 passing.

- /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_19-46-52_claim-office-lite-prose_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts
- /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_19-46-52_claim-office-lite-prose_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts
- /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_19-46-52_claim-office-lite-prose_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/architecture-notes.md

Proceeding to the next test.
